locked_facts: Electric field lines emerge from positive charge, terminate at negative
Field lines NEVER cross each other
Density of field lines is proportional to field strength
Field lines are perpendicular to equipotential surfaces
E = kq/r² for point charge, direction radially outward for +q
Field lines do not represent actual paths of charges
truth_statements: Electric field lines start from positive charges and end at negative charges or extend to infinity
The tangent to a field line at any point gives the direction of electric field at that point
Two field lines can never intersect because the field has a unique direction at every point
The closer the field lines are to each other, the stronger the electric field in that region
For a single positive point charge, the field lines are radially outward and uniformly spaced in 3D

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show equipotential surfaces (wireframe spheres) cutting perpendicular to every field line. Highlight that if lines crossed, the field would have two directions at one point — physically impossible.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Rotate the 3D view so students see field lines passing through equipotential surfaces at 90°. Then try to draw a crossing — it leads to two arrows at one point.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that field lines are a mathematical representation of direction, not paths that charges travel along

simulation_states.label: Single positive charge — radial field lines
simulation_states.description: A positive point charge at the center with field lines radiating outward in all directions. Arrows on lines show direction away from +q.
simulation_states.emphasis: Field lines point AWAY from positive charge — this is the convention

simulation_states.label: Field line density = field strength
simulation_states.description: Camera zooms in closer to the charge. Near the charge, lines are densely packed; far away, they spread out. Caption: denser lines = stronger field.
simulation_states.emphasis: Notice how lines crowd together near the charge — the field is strongest here (E ∝ 1/r²)

simulation_states.label: Equipotential surfaces — perpendicular to lines
simulation_states.description: Wireframe spherical equipotential surfaces appear. Every field line crosses them at exactly 90°. This is the aha moment.
simulation_states.emphasis: Every field line is perpendicular to the equipotential surface — this is always true

simulation_states.label: Explore — rotate and observe
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode: student rotates the 3D view freely. All elements visible. Caption reminds: lines show direction, NOT paths of charges.
simulation_states.emphasis: Rotate to see the full 3D picture. Field lines show direction of force — charges do NOT travel along these lines.

trigger_keywords: field line pe charge chalta hai
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Field lines are actual paths that charges travel along
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Field lines show the DIRECTION of force at each point. The actual path of a charge depends on its initial velocity and other forces. A charge released from rest moves along a field line only for a uniform field — in general, its path curves differently.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show field lines as direction indicators with arrows. Emphasize: these are a map of the force direction, not a road that charges follow.

trigger_keywords: field line ek dusre ko cross
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Field lines can cross each other in certain situations
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: If two field lines crossed, the field would have two directions at the crossing point, which is physically impossible. The electric field has a unique direction at every point in space.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the 3D field and rotate — no matter the angle, lines never touch each other.

typical_question_types: sketch field lines for given charge configuration
find E at a point from field line diagram
identify configuration from field line pattern
common_traps: confusing field lines with charge paths
assuming field line density is constant everywhere
